items.question: What problem does t-SNE solve?
items.answer: t-SNE produces a low-dimensional (usually 2D) embedding that preserves local neighborhoods from high-dimensional data by matching Gaussian input affinities to Student-t output affinities while minimizing KL divergence—primarily for visualization, not feature compression.

items.question: What is perplexity in t-SNE?
items.answer: Perplexity is a hyperparameter that sets the effective number of neighbors each point uses when constructing conditional Gaussian distributions p(j|i); the algorithm adjusts σ_i per point to achieve that entropy level before symmetrizing affinities.

items.question: Why should you not use t-SNE coordinates as ML features?
items.answer: The embedding optimizes a visualization KL objective and distorts global geometry; coordinates are not a consistent, invertible feature map and do not generalize to new points in standard sklearn TSNE—use original or PCA features for modeling.

items.question: How does t-SNE differ from PCA?
items.answer: PCA is linear, preserves global variance along orthogonal components, and supports transform on new data; t-SNE is non-linear, stochastic, preserves local affinities, distorts global distances, and is intended for 2D/3D EDA—link to PCA for linear preprocessing only.

items.question: What preprocessing steps often precede t-SNE?
items.answer: Standardize features when scales differ to balance Euclidean distances; for large p or moderate n, PCA to about 50 components reduces runtime before TSNE—PCA is linear (see PCA/SVD topics); tune perplexity and fix random_state for reproducibility.
